explicit_text: Brunswick, Georgia, is one of the main summer headquarters for the shrimp fishing fleet on the south Atlantic coast. When fishing off the Atlantic coast, we often pack up just the essentials and head to Brunswick, where we rent rooms or a small house and live simply during the season. It's a key location for the fleet before the season shifts to other areas.

explicit_text: Beaufort, South Carolina, along with Brunswick, serves as a summer base for the shrimp fleet on the south Atlantic coast. We usually rent accommodations there and live modestly while the fishing season is active. It's important for us because it marks the seasonal shift in shrimping grounds before we return home for the school year.

explicit_text: Biloxi, Mississippi, is one of the Gulf Coast points where the shrimp fleet heads during the winter months when the shrimp season here is over. Mr. Olsen is starting out for the Gulf coast this time, which includes places like Biloxi, Morgan City, and Galveston. These locations are crucial for following the shrimp migration and continuing the catch through different seasons.

explicit_text: Morgan City, Louisiana, is another Gulf Coast location where the shrimp fleet migrates during the winter season. Along with Biloxi and Galveston, it serves as a key fishing ground when shrimp become scarce on the Atlantic coast. We follow the shrimp migration carefully, moving our boats to places like Morgan City to keep up with the best catch.

explicit_text: Galveston, Texas, is one of the Gulf Coast points where the shrimp fleet relocates during the winter months. When the shrimp season here ends, we head to Galveston along with other places like Biloxi and Morgan City to continue fishing. It’s part of the seasonal cycle that keeps the shrimp industry going throughout the year.

explicit_text: Fernandina Beach, Florida, is where the shrimp industry as we know it began around 1913. It was the starting point for the development of shrimp fishing in this region before places like St. Augustine grew into important centers. I find it fascinating how this small town played such a pivotal role in the early days of the industry.

explicit_text: I was born near the St. Johns River and have spent almost all my life in St. Augustine, Florida. The city has a well-protected harbor and the San Sebastian River offers safe anchorage for many small shrimp boats during the season. St. Augustine became a thriving shrimping center with packing houses and a canning factory, and our local shrimp dishes have even gained a reputation among visitors.

explicit_text: The San Sebastian River in St. Augustine provides safe anchorage for many small shrimp boats during the fishing season. It’s a narrow river but very important for the fleet that has its headquarters here. The river supports the local shrimp industry by offering a secure place for the boats to stay while the season is active.

explicit_text: New York City is the main distributing point for the fresh shrimp we catch here in St. Augustine. Our shrimp are shipped there mostly in refrigerated trucks to ensure quick delivery to northern markets. It’s interesting to know that the price there is about ten cents per pound wholesale, with the heads removed before shipping.

explicit_text: My husband, Gus, was born in Sweden but has lived here since 1920. He is a Swede by birth, and his background is part of the diverse mix of nationalities involved in the shrimp industry, including Italians, Spanish, Portuguese, and Greeks. Gus is a good husband and father figure to our children, and we are proud of our family life here.

explicit_text: We are Democrats and always vote at election time. Our interest in politics extends to local civic government and the national government, especially regarding working conditions. We are also deeply concerned about local issues like harbor improvement, which affects the safety and success of our shrimping operations.
